stories.headline: FOMC OPENS APRIL MEETING TOMORROW WITH OIL-DRIVEN CPI AT 3.3% — POWELL'S WEDNESDAY GUIDANCE IS THE REAL EVENT
stories.body: The Fed begins its April 28–29 meeting with the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75%; markets price a 99.9% probability of a hold. March CPI re-accelerated to 3.3% YoY (vs. 2.4% prior), well above target, driven largely by $107 Brent crude and energy pass-through into transportation costs. Wednesday's 2:30 PM ET Powell press conference will be parsed for whether the pause extends into 2027 or a 2026 cut remains alive.

stories.headline: BRENT SURGES TO $107, WTI TO $95.50 AS U.S.-IRAN TALKS STALL AND STRAIT OF HORMUZ REMAINS AT RISK
stories.body: Stalled U.S.-Iran peace negotiations and effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz — through which ~20% of global seaborne oil transits — sent Brent crude up 3.8% to $107/bbl Monday, its highest level since 2022. Every $10/bbl move in crude adds ~20-25 bps to headline U.S. CPI within 60-90 days, meaning the current spike alone contributes roughly 50 bps of incremental inflation pressure directly into the Fed's target overshoot.

stories.headline: S&P 500 AND NASDAQ NOTCH ALL-TIME HIGHS AT 7,165 AND 24,025 AS MICROSOFT ENDS EXCLUSIVE OPENAI PARTNERSHIP
stories.body: Equities hit fresh records Monday despite oil-driven inflation concerns, with Microsoft's announcement it is ending its exclusive partnership with OpenAI boosting broad AI sector sentiment. The S&P 500 edged up +0.11% to 7,165 and the Nasdaq gained +0.19% to 24,025; the Dow underperformed, dragged by transportation and consumer discretionary stocks sensitive to energy costs. A split tape — tech up, economically-sensitive names down — is the signature of an oil supply shock.

stories.headline: IMF'S APRIL 2026 WORLD ECONOMIC OUTLOOK CUTS GLOBAL GROWTH AS TARIFF RESIDUALS AND ENERGY SPIKE COMPOUND
stories.body: The IMF's April 2026 WEO, titled 'Global Economy Tested Again,' trimmed its 2026 global growth baseline, citing tariff-driven friction (still responsible for roughly half of excess CPI above 2%) and the new energy supply shock. The IMF projects core PCE edging toward 2% in H2 2026 only if oil normalizes — a condition now deeply in doubt with the Strait of Hormuz disruption showing no near-term resolution.

stories.headline: COLLEGE HOOPS OFFSEASON IN MOTION: NBA DRAFT DECLARATIONS FINALIZED, TRANSFER PORTAL RESHAPING ROSTERS FOR 2026-27
stories.body: Three weeks after Michigan defeated UConn 69-63 to claim the 2026 NCAA Championship — the Big Ten's first title since Michigan State in 2000 — rosters across college basketball continue to shift. NBA Draft early entry deadlines have passed, and programs are filling holes through the transfer portal. Washington added transfers LeJuan Watts (Texas Tech) and Steele Venters (Gonzaga) this week as programs firm up their 2026-27 rosters.

stories.headline: SPURS MAKE NBA PLAYOFF HISTORY WITH +15-HALFTIME-DEFICIT TO +15-WIN SWING; CELTICS CRUSH 76ERS 128-96 FOR 3-1 LEAD
stories.body: San Antonio became the first team in NBA playoff history to trail by 15+ points at halftime and win by 15+ points, as De'Aaron Fox (28 pts) and Victor Wembanyama (27 pts, 7 BLK, 4 STL) led a stunning Game 4 reversal. In the East, Payton Pritchard's playoff career-high 32 off the bench — alongside Jayson Tatum's 30 pts, 11 AST — powered Boston to a 128-96 dismantling of Philadelphia, giving the Celtics a commanding 3-1 series lead.

stories.headline: 2026 NFL DRAFT GRADES ARRIVE: CHIEFS AND BUCS EARN TOP MARKS; FERNANDO MENDOZA ERA BEGINS IN LAS VEGAS
stories.body: The 2026 NFL Draft concluded Saturday in Pittsburgh with IU quarterback Fernando Mendoza going No. 1 overall to Las Vegas — and nine offensive linemen taken in Round 1. CBS Sports and NBC Sports award the Chiefs and Buccaneers top draft grades; the Jaguars drew sharp criticism. Teams are now in the undrafted free agent window, with all 32 rosters building toward the 90-man offseason roster limit.

stories.headline: TRANSFER QB MARKET HITS RECORD NIL PRICES AS SPRING PORTAL CLOSES; OKLAHOMA STATE LEADS FBS WITH 50 INCOMING
stories.body: ESPN reports college football's spring transfer portal cycle set records for quarterback NIL costs, with top signal-callers commanding multi-million dollar packages. Oklahoma State leads all FBS programs with 50 incoming transfers under new coach Eric Morris from North Texas. Oregon locked in Dylan Raiola via the portal while LSU's pursuit of ESPN's No. 3 transfer Sam Leavitt — over Miami and Tennessee — headlined the most consequential moves for 2026 title contenders.

sports.detail: vs. Orlando Magic, 8 PM ET. Magic lead series 2-1. #1 seed (60-22) in historic upset danger — must-win.
At Twins, 7:40 PM ET (Target Field). Record 14-15 after 11-9 win vs. STL on Sunday. Series runs Apr 27-29.
Drafted RB Jadarian Price (Notre Dame) #32 overall. Lost SB MVP RB Kenneth Walker III to KC. Now signing UDFAs.
Demond Williams Jr. holds QB1 role. Roster returns 297 combined starts from 2025 LA Bowl. 4-star WR Tre Moore visiting campus.
Added G LeJuan Watts (Texas Tech, Apr 25) and G Steele Venters (Gonzaga). 10 of 15 roster spots filled for 2026-27.

watchlist: FOMC rate decision Wednesday Apr 29, 2 PM ET — hold certain; Powell press conference 2:30 PM ET is the real signal on inflation duration
Q1 2026 GDP advance estimate Thursday Apr 30 — oil shock may weigh on real growth even as nominal stays elevated
Core PCE Friday May 1 — Fed's preferred inflation gauge; elevated print could extinguish remaining 2026 cut expectations
Pistons vs. Magic Game 5 — Wednesday Apr 29, 7 PM ET if Detroit wins tonight; series on the line
Mariners at Twins series — Apr 27-29 at Target Field; M's (14-15) need wins to stay in AL West conversation

deepDive.title: The Fed's Oil-Shock Policy Bind: Why Rate Hikes Can't Fix a Supply Problem — and Why That's Dangerous in 2026
content: Today's S&P 500 record at 7,165 sits alongside $107 Brent crude and a 3.3% headline CPI — a combination that captures the Federal Reserve's central dilemma heading into tomorrow's FOMC meeting. The Strait of Hormuz crisis and stalled US-Iran negotiations have pushed energy costs to multiyear highs just as the Fed had hoped its long pause at 3.50-3.75% would allow inflation to drift back toward 2%. Markets price a 99.9% probability of a hold on Wednesday, but the real question is not whether Powell holds — it's what he signals about duration, and whether he acknowledges that the primary inflation driver is a supply shock that monetary policy cannot cure.

The transmission mechanism from oil to CPI runs in two channels. The direct channel is arithmetic: energy is roughly 7% of CPI, and every $10/barrel move in crude adds approximately 20-25 basis points to headline inflation within 60-90 days. With Brent up ~$22 from early-2026 levels, that alone contributes 45-55 bps of mechanical upward pressure before any secondary effects. The indirect channel unfolds over 3-6 months: transportation costs in trucking, airlines, and shipping reprice higher, feeding into core goods and services with a lag. What begins as energy CPI becomes stickier core inflation — the worst outcome for a Fed trying to declare victory on price stability without triggering a recession.

The canonical mistake in supply-shock monetary policy is the Fed's response to the 1973 OPEC embargo: aggressive tightening into a supply disruption deepened recession without fully resolving inflation, because rate hikes suppress demand but cannot create oil supply. The 1990 Gulf War spike — when Brent surged roughly 90% in four months — offered a more successful template: the Fed held steady, the shock faded in ~six months, and core inflation normalized. The critical variable in both cases was whether inflation expectations remained anchored. In 1973 they broke; in 1990 they held. Today's 5-year breakeven inflation rates hovering around 2.7% and deteriorating consumer survey confidence suggest expectations are under pressure — which is precisely why the Fed cannot simply 'look through' this shock the way classical theory prescribes.

The 2026 setup introduces two complications absent from prior analogues. First, the Fed has been on hold for multiple consecutive meetings with inflation already above target — each passing meeting without progress erodes the credibility of the 'temporary pause' narrative and raises the risk that markets reprice to a persistently higher-for-longer baseline, lifting long-end yields and compressing equity multiples. Second, Powell's term as chair is ending soon, creating institutional uncertainty about the Fed's 2% commitment that the bond market will independently price into inflation expectations. If Wednesday's statement leans hawkish — emphasizing inflation persistence — expect the 2-year Treasury yield to rise and 2026 cut bets to shift to 2027. If Powell frames this as a supply-side shock and preserves H2 2026 optionality, equities stay bid. Watch the 2-year yield in the first ten minutes after 2 PM ET: that move tells you which scenario the bond market believes.
